label: 204_marriage_cohabiting_married_married parents
description: The share of young adults in marriages has sharply declined since the 1960s. Stable families benefit children economically and socially, but there's a growing class gap in marital status that may affect social mobility. Researchers explore why married parents often raise more successful children.

earliest_articles.title: How Marriage Survives
earliest_articles.excerpt: The recession has taken a toll on the institution of marriage, we keep hearing. Last month, for instance, when it was reported that the proportion of Americans aged 25 to 34 who are married fell below the proportion who have never married, it was quickly attributed to the economic downturn. Young adults, according to…

earliest_articles.title: Strengthening Fragile Families
earliest_articles.excerpt: The rise of fragile families—families that begin when a child is born outside of marriage—is one of the nation’s most vexing social problems. In the first place, these families suffer high poverty rates and poor child outcomes. Even more problematic, the very groups of Americans who traditionally experience poverty,…

earliest_articles.title: The Marginalization of Marriage in Middle America
earliest_articles.excerpt: Abstract This policy brief reviews the deepening marginalization of marriage and the growing instability of family life among moderately-educated Americans: those who hold high school degrees but not four-year college degrees and who constitute 51 percent of the young adult population (aged twenty-five to…

newest_articles.title: Key takeaways from the discussion on “The Two-Parent Privilege”
newest_articles.excerpt: This article is a summary of the discussion at an event hosted by the Center for Economic Security and Opportunity on September 21, 2023. You can watch the full event here. newest_articles.title: The elephant in the room
newest_articles.excerpt: more comfortable to talk about elements of inequality, threats to social mobility, and policy interventions that are outside the orbit of family relationships, we need to discuss this metaphorical elephant in the room. newest_articles.title: The “College Gap” in Marriage and Children’s Family Structure
newest_articles.excerpt: The “College Gap” in Marriage and Children’s Family Structure The share of children living in a two-parent family has declined sharply in the past 40 years, driven by a decline in marriage among parents without a four-year college degree. This paper presents a number of facts about these trends, drawing on US Census…

central_articles.title: How to Save Marriage in America
central_articles.excerpt: What’s happening to American matrimony? In 1960, more than 70 percent of all adults were married, including nearly six in ten twentysomethings. Half a century later, just 20 percent of 18-29-year olds were hitched in 2010. Marriage was the norm for young America. Now it’s the exception. American marriage is not dying.…

central_articles.title: Cohabiting parents differ from married ones in three big ways
central_articles.excerpt: Here are two important, largely uncontested facts: - Family stability is important for childhood outcomes. All else equal, children raised in stable families are healthier, better educated, and more likely to avoid poverty than those who experience transitions in family structure.1 - Married parents are more likely to…

central_articles.title: The marriage effect: Money or parenting?
central_articles.excerpt: There is a growing marriage gap along class lines in America. This may be bad news for social mobility, since children raised by married parents typically do better in life on almost every available economic and social measure. But it is important to try and understand why the children of married parents do better. Is…
